story:
Larry Schmidt, assistant to Ernest Haptig, PhD, was ready to take the greatest risk of his life to repay a debt he felt he owed. Haptig had brought him into a national laboratory, mentored him patiently despite Schmidt’s thin scientific background, and even made him co-author on papers born entirely of Haptig’s insight and labor. After years of dissecting Einstein’s work and failing forward through false starts, Haptig had done the impossible: he had found a way to treat time like a spatial dimension—to move not only forward but backward under very specific conditions.

Together they built a machine to prove it. They worked day and night, poured in their savings, and reached the moment when theory would meet reality. Schmidt wanted to be the first inside, but Haptig refused to risk a human life until inanimate trials and animal tests proved harmless. They began with a block of wood. When the machine activated, the block vanished. That was either triumph or disaster; the theory said “time travel,” but the lab could not tell whether the block had gone somewhere—or nowhere.

They devised tests, some harebrained, some plausible, to get unambiguous evidence. At last they settled on a clean idea: send a small box one year into the future with the machine’s experimental spatial-correction field engaged, along with a note asking their future selves to send the box back to prove success. The box disappeared, just as the wood had. They realized they had not specified a precise return moment, but assumed their future selves would consult the lab notebooks and choose a safe, later time. They waited.

Days turned into weeks. Haptig disassembled and rebuilt the machine, rechecked the theory, hunted errors that did not appear. The box did not return. Haptig’s energy drained away. Gray hair spread, his shoulders stooped, and hope thinned to nothing. At last, hollow-eyed, he whispered that perhaps the theory was wrong.

Watching his mentor crumble, Schmidt could not bear it. The world, he believed, needed Haptig’s brilliance, and proof would save him. Schmidt decided to become the evidence. The future felt dangerous—what if the world changed in ways he could not predict, or Haptig was gone before Schmidt arrived? The past seemed safer. Yesterday was known. If he arrived one day earlier and predicted events, he would be living, undeniable proof that Haptig had bent time to his will.

He chose a conservative jump: back one day. He convinced himself it was careful. He did not account for a crucial fact. The box experiment had depended on the machine’s still-unfinished spatial correction, a stabilizing field Haptig had improvised for unmanned trials. Schmidt, impatient and working alone, bypassed it to simplify the human jump. The machine moved its contents in time, not in space. A day earlier, the laboratory would be in a different place—not just rotated beneath the sun, but far along Earth’s orbit, and Earth itself racing with the sun through the galaxy. The planet would not be where it was. Schmidt set the controls, stepped into the machine, and activated it.

In an instant he arrived one day earlier at the same coordinates in space, but the Earth had moved on. There was no laboratory around him, no air, only vacuum. If he had a moment to think before the cold and emptiness took him, he might have grasped the scale of his error: time can be treated like a dimension, but travel demands reckoning with space as well. His sacrifice proved nothing to the world, and left behind the man he wanted to save.
